name: Chilean Maule Valley (Andean foothills, ~800m)

staple_foods.name: Golden Delicious apple

primary_metabolites.fructose_concentration: 5.2-6.8 g/100g [Chilean Maule apple — Vial et al. 2019 Chilean J Agric Res; standard Golden Delicious range]
primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 0.12-0.20 g/100g [Chilean highland apple 700-900m — Vial et al. 2019; UV stress at Andean foothills]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.38-0.50 g/100g [Golden Delicious, Maule Andean foothills 700-900m — Vial et al. 2019 Chilean J Agric Res; intermediate altitude, moderate malic retention vs Shanxi highlands]

staple_foods.notes: Golden Delicious apple grown at Andean foothills (~800m) in Chile's Maule Valley — the world's largest apple export region. Volcanic Andean soils, Mediterranean climate with summer drought. Intermediate altitude between Shanxi lowlands (600m, malic=0.25) and Shanxi highlands (1050m, malic=0.505). Expected malic acid 0.38-0.50 g/100g — higher than Shanxi lowlands but lower than Shanxi highlands. Ascorbic acid elevated vs RSU-13/04 due to high UV at Andean foothills. Note: different region from Shanxi comparison (Zhao et al. 2021) — between-study variance expected; nevertheless same cultivar and altitude mechanism applies.
data_sources: Vial et al. (2019) — Physicochemical characterization of apple varieties grown in Chilean Maule Valley, Chilean J Agric Res 79(2)
Zhao et al. (2021) — Effects of genetic background and altitude on apple metabolites, Foods 10(12):2950

staple_foods.name: wheat bread (pan de molde / marraqueta)
staple_foods.notes: Marraqueta (Chilean crusty roll) is the iconic Chilean bread — per capita bread consumption in Chile is among the highest globally. Placed in fat slot as placeholder; avocado oil is the more interesting fat for this RSU but data gap exists. Maule is also a major wine region — avocado not dominant here.

staple_foods.name: beef (Maule Andean foothill cattle)
staple_foods.notes: Chilean Maule Valley beef from semi-extensive Andean foothill grazing systems.

staple_foods.name: merkén (smoked chili-coriander spice blend)

key_flavor_bioactives.capsaicinoids: 0.5-2.5 mg/g capsaicin equivalent [merkén — mild smoked Mapuche chili; Capsicum annuum var. — Ihl et al. 2003 Pesq Agropec Bras]

terpenes.linalool: present — coriander seed component of traditional merkén blend

staple_foods.notes: Merkén is the traditional Mapuche smoked chili blend — dried, smoked cacho de cabra peppers ground with coriander seed and salt. Distinctly smoky, mildly spicy. The defining aromatic of southern Chilean indigenous cuisine and increasingly adopted in modern Chilean cooking.
data_sources: Ihl et al. (2003) — Characterization of merkén, Pesq Agropec Bras

staple_foods.name: chicha de uva (Maule grape fermented must)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 200-600 mg GAE/100mL [Maule chicha de uva — rough estimate from grape must fermentation; higher in red grape varieties]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 1.5-4.0 g/L [Maule grape must — tartaric and malic dominant; Chilean oenological literature]
organic_acids.lactic_acid: trace in primary fermentation; elevated in malolactic fermentation

staple_foods.notes: Chicha de uva (fermented grape must) is the traditional fermented drink of Maule — a semi-alcoholic, cloudy grape ferment distinct from wine. Maule Valley is Chile's largest wine production region (Cabernet Sauvignon, Carmenère, País). Chicha represents the artisanal, low-intervention ferment that predates industrial winemaking.

notes: Chilean Maule Valley Andean foothills (~800m). Volcanic Andean soils, Mediterranean climate. Key natural experiment RSU: Golden Delicious apple at 800m — fills the intermediate altitude slot between Shanxi lowland (RSU-13, 600m, malic=0.25) and Shanxi highland (RSU-14, 1050m, malic=0.505). Expected malic acid 0.38-0.50 g/100g. Natural experiment partner: RSU-13, RSU-14 (Shanxi altitude experiment), RSU-50 (Turkish Isparta, 1100m).
